nativeRange.habitat: Tropical reef flats, lagoons, and shallow rocky substrates across the Indo-Pacific, where juveniles and adults graze biofilm and algae on hard surfaces from the low intertidal zone down to roughly 20 m depth.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with established algae film
stable rock structures (large snails can bulldoze loose rock)

diet.notes: The Trochus Snail is a voracious grazer on film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and soft green algae across glass, live rock, and substrate. It is generally considered effective against film and diatom outbreaks but does not reliably clear long hair algae or macroalgae. When the tank's algae supply is depleted, dried nori or seaweed sheets and sinking herbivore wafers can be offered as a supplement. Adequate calcium (around 400–450 ppm) and magnesium (around 1250–1350 ppm) are needed to maintain shell integrity.

goodWith: other cleanup crew snails (Astraea, Nassarius, Ceriths)
avoidWith: predatory wrasses (Coris, Thalassoma) that crush snails

summary: The Trochus Snail is widely regarded as one of the most reliable saltwater cleanup-crew snails, valued for its ability to right itself when knocked over — an advantage over Astraea snails, which cannot.
careNotes:
The Trochus Snail is an umbrella name for several closely related species in the family Trochidae sold under the genus name Trochus in the reef hobby — most commonly Trochus radiatus and Trochus histrio, along with Tectus niloticus, which is still widely marketed under its older name Trochus niloticus or as the Banded Trochus despite having been reclassified to the genus Tectus. All share essentially identical care requirements in the reef aquarium: they have pyramid-shaped shells, graze on film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and soft green algae, and thrive in stable reef chemistry (pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, specific gravity 1.023–1.025, temperature 23–26 °C).

A notable husbandry advantage over the similar-looking Astraea Snail (Astraea tecta) is that Trochus snails can right themselves when knocked over. Astraea snails cannot, and may starve if left upside down without intervention — making Trochus generally the more forgiving cleanup-crew choice. Like all marine invertebrates, they are intolerant of copper-based medications.

Captive-bred lines are available from aquaculture facilities such as ORA and generally arrive in better condition than wild-collected stock. Stocking density is commonly cited as one snail per 8–12 L (roughly 2–3 US gallons) of display volume; overstocking risks starvation once the available algae is depleted. Adults are capable of dislodging loose rockwork, so aquascaping should be well secured before adding them.
breedingNotes: Trochus snails are broadcast spawners, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column where fertilization occurs. Larvae have an unusually short planktonic phase of roughly 3–7 days before settling, which makes Trochus relatively practical for commercial aquaculture compared to most marine gastropods, and captive-bred juveniles are available from aquaculture producers such as ORA. Hobbyist-scale breeding in display tanks is rare, but spontaneous settlement has been reported in mature reef systems.
